first_line: AI startups have 12 months before they die…
post_full:
AI startups have 12 months before they die…

Every week I watch a category shrink.

The pattern is always the same.

Small team ships a clever AI wrapper.
Founders raise a round.
Six months later, Anthropic or OpenAI ships the same thing natively.

The startup pivots or dies quietly.

Most people I talk to are building the exact thing OpenAI will ship next quarter. They just don't see it yet.

The ones who survive are building moats the platforms can't copy.

There are only three that still hold:
- Distribution
- Proprietary data
- Deep workflow integration

Canva has 100 million users. When they decide your AI feature belongs in their workflow, you're not competing against their product. 

You're competing against their audience.

That's distribution.

Customer support logs from 2023 aren't a moat. Real-time workflow data from power users who can't leave is.

That's proprietary data.

And deep integration isn't "we added AI to our app." It's going so far into the job that switching means rebuilding the whole workflow from scratch.

That's the third.

Everything else is a feature waiting to be absorbed.

Notion rebuilt their agent system five times before shipping.

Every time they got close, a platform provider shipped the feature underneath them.

They only won by going deeper, not wider.

I doubt current stakes are about model access anymore. That war is over.

IMO, it's about who owns the user when the feature becomes commodity.

Small teams can still win.

But "build the feature, they'll come" is a 2023 strategy running on 2026 timelines.

what are you building that only YOU can build?

first_line: Every SaaS is shipping an AI button right now. Most of them are going to look ridiculous in 18 months.
post_full:
Every SaaS is shipping an AI button right now. Most of them are going to look ridiculous in 18 months.

- Notion has one.
- Linear has one.
- Slack has one.

Every tool in my stack now has a little sparkle icon in the corner.
Click it, chat box opens, "hi, how can i help?"

The button isn't the problem.
The user it's designed for is.

Every AI button ships with the same unstated assumption: a human is inside the app, clicking around, and the AI's job is to make that human faster.

But that's the part that's changing.

When we build agents at Atlan, we don't have them click buttons.
- They call APIs.
- They read from databases.
- They talk to other apps through MCP's
-  They do the whole job and post the result someplace we want

The human never opens the app.

Some headlines from this week to back my hypothesis: 

Figma's bet: designers stay in Figma, AI makes them faster inside it.

Anthropic's bet: designers don't open Figma. They describe what they want to Claude.

Same user. Two very different futures.

OpenAI's Codex update shipped computer use (the agent literally sees your screen and operates your apps for you), an in-app browser, and 90+ plugins, many of them MCP servers.

The head of Codex said it out loud: "we're building the superapp in the open."

IMO, here's what most product teams aren't thinking yet:

- You're not in a race to make your app AI-powered. 
- You're in a race against the interaction layer itself shifting.
- The question isn't "did we add AI to our product? 
- It's "who's going to be using our product 18 months from now.

If the answer is an agent, the sparkle button is aimed at a user who's already walking out the door.

The things that actually matter when your user is an agent:

-  .md files
- Clean, well-documented APIs and MCP servers
- Clear approval gates where a human stays in the loop
- Outputs that drop into the channels humans actually live in
- The data and logic an agent can't easily replicate somewhere else

None of that is a button.
None of it ships in a corner of your nav bar.

I'm not saying UIs die.

Humans will still want nice interfaces for the work they still do directly.

But a huge chunk of software was built on the assumption that the human is the operator.

That assumption is breaking (quietly).

The companies that see it early will build for the new user.

The ones that don't will ship a prettier sparkle button next quarter and wonder why engagement is flat.

Add AI to your product, sure.
But ask the harder question first: who's actually going to be using this thing?

Because they might not be who you think.

first_line: Someone raised $9M just to babysit AI-generated code.
post_full:
Someone raised $9M just to babysit AI-generated code.

Not to write it. Not to improve it. To audit what agents spit out.

The startup Gitar secured funding on this exact thesis. Agents are now writing enough production code that securing it is a venture-scale problem.

Same week, OpenAI shipped sandboxed execution and long-running agent support. The container management and state persistence builders have been duct-taping for months? Platform-level now.

These two things look unrelated. They're not.

This is multiple layers of the agent stack hardening at once:
- runtime primitives becoming first-class
- orchestration getting native support
- security tools catching up to output volume

And the big software companies? Creative tools, productivity suites, dev platforms. All moving from single-app AI features to multi-step agent workflows.

Notion's cofounder shared they rebuilt their agent system five times before it worked. 100+ tools. Tradeoffs with no clear answers.

At Atlan, we've been building agents for months and this tracks.

The results can be magical. But "magical" still costs you hours of fighting context management, output quality, hallucinations, edge cases, and latency.

Here's why IMO that cost should drop now:
- native sandboxed execution = stop building infra
- long-running support = stop managing state yourself
- security tooling = stop manually auditing agent output

Each primitive that stabilizes removes a full layer of pain from your stack.
Waiting for v2 of everything sounds rational. But the teams shipping today are the ones defining what v2 looks like.

Muscle memory of building multi-step, multi-tool agent systems compounds in ways that reading changelogs never will.

What's the ugliest workaround in your current agent setup? 👀

first_line: I finally broke GPT…
post_full:
I finally broke GPT…
I asked how many r’s are in “strawberry.”

It said 2.

Meanwhile a five-year-old 
“laughing” and “counting”

S-T-R-A-W-B-E-R-R-Y. (3.)

This trillion-dollar technology that can write code, pass medical exams, and explain quantum physics... 

Can't count to 3.

Why?

LLMs are “like” your phone’s autocomplete

You type: “I’m heading to the…”
Your phone: Gym, store, office.

It does’t access your calendar, rather completes a common pattern.

LLMs do the same. 
So when you ask, “How many r’s in strawberry?” 
It’s trying to guess or predict a number

Words with three of the same letter are uncommon, so the model goes: “Probably 1… maybe 2.”

Elephant memory, goldfish math.

Meanwhile we ask AI to:
→ Analyze customer data
→ Count sales trends
→ Track inventory

…and it’s out here failing kindergarten.

So what’s the fix?
→ Make it list things first vs direct counting
→ Use code for counting. "strawberry".count("r") → 3
→ Double-check. “Do it twice independently; if different, recount.”
→ Force proof. “Line 1: the number. Line 2: the positions you used.”

Quick test:

“How many p’s in hippopotamus?”
Correct: 3.
ChatGPT (too often): “Definitely 2.”

This is honestly the funniest thing I’ve learned all year.

Here’s a small explainer on this topic ↓

first_line: Your AI startup isn't a SaaS company.
post_full:
Your AI startup isn't a SaaS company.

It’s an all-you-can-eat buffet where your customers get twice as hungry every 6 months.

The $20/month flat-fee pricing model is a trap.

Founders are betting the farm on a simple assumption:
As compute gets cheaper, our margins will grow.

This is a fatal oversimplification.

It ignores the other side of the equation:
As AI gets more powerful, our appetite for it explodes.

Last year, a user was happy summarizing a 1-page doc.

Today, they want to analyze a 200-page PDF and ask 50 follow-up questions.

Your costs for that user just went up 100x.
But the cost of compute didn't drop 100x.
And even if it did, our ambition would just expand 1000x to meet it.

You're left holding the bag.

So what's the playbook? 

In his piece on this, Ethan Ding suggests three paths to survive:

1. Usage-Based Pricing: 
Stop selling a buffet. Charge for every plate.

2. High Switching Costs: 
Integrate deeply into a customer's workflow (especially enterprise) that leaving becomes too painful.

3. Vertical Integration: 
Make the AI a loss-leader for other, highly profitable services like hosting or databases.

The common thread is you must build a business model that profits from explosive use, instead of being drowned by it.

Which path are you choosing?

first_line: I asked AI one simple question about our future...
post_full:
I asked AI one simple question about our future...

The answer was terrifying

My prompt was simple:

"Based on how we operate as humans today, create an image showing humanity in 50 years if we continue this behavior"

AI had infinite futures to choose from.

Medical miracles. Space colonies. Green cities. Mundane progress. Radical transformation.

It chose dystopia.

Respirator masks. Scorched skies. Dead rivers. Digital billboards selling fake happiness.

Every sci-fi nightmare, perfectly rendered.

But here's what's surprising:

I never asked for dystopia.

AI just assumed that's what I meant.

Because that's what we've taught it "the future" means.

Through every:
- Climate headline we share
- Apocalypse movie we stream
- Doomsday prediction we click
- Black Mirror episode we binge

We've trained AI that future equals fear.

The most advanced prediction engine ever built can only imagine one tomorrow:

The one where everything breaks.
Not because that's inevitable.
Because that's all we feed it.

What does it say about us
when our machines can't dream
of anything but nightmares?

first_line: My mom asked AI to cancel her dentist appointment.
post_full:
My mom asked AI to cancel her dentist appointment.

It wrote a LinkedIn message.

147 words.
3 paragraphs.
"I am writing to formally request..."

She deleted it all.

Typed: "Can't make Saturday"

Sent.

That's when it hit me.

Every AI tool today is broken the same way:

- Gmail AI turns 5 words into 50
- Notion AI explains your notes back to you
- Calendar AI suggests meetings nobody wants
- Grammarly makes everyone sound like robots

We put rocket engines on bicycles.
Then wonder why we keep falling over.

Here's the thing:

Instead of: "AI, help me write emails"
Should be: "AI, handle all my emails"

Instead of: "AI, summarize this doc"
Should be: "AI, just show me what matters"

Instead of: "AI, improve my writing"
Should be: "AI, write like me so I don't have to"

See the difference?

Current AI = Faster way to do boring work
Real AI = Never do boring work again

Mom doesn't want a smarter assistant.

She wants her appointment canceled.
In 5 words.
Like she asked.

AI should do what we want, how we want it, without us having to manage it.

All she wanted was a simple email. AI made it complex.

But we'll get there.
We always do.

BTW - Mom asked if she can train Gmail by yelling at it like Siri. I said no. She said "Then what's the point?"

Mom: 2 | AI: 0

p.s. inspired by Pete Koomen's "AI Horseless Carriages" - a must-read on why we're building AI wrong

first_line: Last week I discovered I'm terrible
post_full:
Last week I discovered I'm terrible
at the one thing I pride myself on:

Clear communication.

The evidence was overwhelming.

I literally get paid to make 
complex AI workflows simple.

Then I wrote a "simple" team announcement.

Enter Suren.

"Nice announcement. But how many
things did you assume people know?"

I thought "Maybe 5-6 things?"

"Let's check."

We asked AI to count.
Every. Single. Assumption.

36.

Thirty-six assumptions. In one message.

Like believing people:
- Know what "n8n" means
- Will brainstorm great use cases
- See why AI enablement matters
- Understand what are AI workflows
(and more.....)

My "clear" message?
Cryptic.

Written in a language
only I understand.

Here's what hit me:

We're all doing this.
Every email. Every presentation.
Every "simple" explanation.

The curse of knowledge is real.

The more you know,
the worse you communicate.

You literally forget
what it's like to "not" know.

So here's my question:

What obvious thing do you say daily that's probably not obvious at all?

first_line: I read something nasty today 😔
post_full:
I read something nasty today 😔

And I can't stop thinking about it.

Apparently, we lost the internet to bots.

According to the 2025 Imperva Bad Bot report:
- 51% of all web traffic is now bots
- 37% of them are malicious
- Up from 32% last year

First time ever we're the minority on the Internet.

Yesterday Sam Parr posted "LinkedIn should ban AI comments".

IMO, AI comments aren't the real problem.
They're just the part we can see.

The real issue? We're optimizing for all the wrong things:
- That engagement metric you track? Mostly fake.
- That traffic spike you celebrated? Probably bots..
- That growth curve you show investors? Built on lies...

We built the internet for connection.

Handed it to the machines instead.
Now we perform for an audience that doesn't exist.

Want to know if you're talking to humans?

Check these patterns in your analytics:
- Geographic distribution that's too perfect
- Session durations that don't vary
- Traffic spikes with no real source
- Engagement at weird hours

Or don't.

The bots don't care either way.

They've already won and the jokes on us.

first_line: I'm convinced some teenager with ChatGPT is destroying my returns.
post_full:
I'm convinced some teenager with ChatGPT is destroying my returns.

And it's keeping me up at night.

I spent 67 hours analyzing Phoenix Mills.
Bought at ₹940.
It's up 69%.

But somewhere, some kid probably typed "good stocks" and did better.

While I'm here:
- Copying whatever Akshat does
- Reading random reports at 2 AM
- Pretending I understand why it works

(I don't. I just have conviction and Excel.)

So when Zerodha dropped their Kite MCP, I did something:

Started feeding it GOLD:
- Berkshire annual letters
- Munger's mental models
- Mark Minervini's technical systems

Building an AI that thinks like I wish I could.

The uncomfortable truth?
That teenager doesn't overthink.
They just ask: "what will go up?"

While I ask: "what's the debt-to-equity ratio adjusted for seasonal fluctuations?"

Maybe that's what I'm really building -
A way to ask simple questions again.

Teaching AI to be obsessed so I don't have to be.

What weird thing are you building that no one knows about?

P.S. The AI hasn't made a single trade yet. But it did suggest I "try index funds." Even my AI thinks I overthink.

first_line: every startup says they're "ai-powered" now.
post_full:
every startup says they're "ai-powered" now.

but 99% are just chatgpt wrappers with extra steps.

the 1% though? they're terrifying.

Don't get me WRONG, the 99% aren't dumb. 
Smart companies. Great founders. Wrong approach.

Here's why:

They're still thinking AI First when winners are going AI Native.

Most companies don't even know there's a difference.

There is. And it changes everything.

See, there are two types of companies right now:

AI First: "How can we add AI to improve X?" 
AI Native: "Why does X exist at all?"

One optimizes. One eliminates.

At Atlan, we realized going AI Native means transforming at 3 levels:

- AI Native Departments: Reimagining what Marketing, Sales, Product even do
- AI Native Talent: Every single person thinking with AI, not just using it
- AI Native Operations: Breaking down silos, AI-powered collaboration

Khosla Ventures (the $15 Bn VC) gets us:
They're buying call centers and rebuilding them AI Native.
Not improving them. Replacing their entire existence.

The uncomfortable truth:
If you're adding AI features, you're playing defense. 
If you're eliminating human constraints, you're playing offense.

Most of your processes exist because humans have limitations:
- Can't work 24/7
- Can't predict what they don't know
- Can't process 1000 data points simultaneously

Kill the constraint. Kill the process.

Ask yourself: What would you never have built if AI always existed?

Your answer might be your biggest opportunity.

Or your biggest threat if someone else figures it out first.

What would you delete if AI had always existed?

p.s. My honest answer? My entire role. That's why I'm learning to build what replaces me. 
p.p.s This is me drooling over our new N8N setup 👇🏻

first_line: Last week, my manager asked me:
post_full:
Last week, my manager asked me:

Aayush, are you making "progress", or just creating the "illusion" of it?

I'd spent the last 5 years being an "execution machine." This question broke me.

See, before Atlan, I was the guy who:
- Shipped things fast
- Checked off 10+ tasks before lunch
- Worked mindlessly and felt productive

I wore my “busyness” like a badge of honor.

Then I joined Atlan. Showed my first week's progress.

My manager's response?

"You did A thing”. That's motion, not progress. 
Actual progress? What metric did it move for us? 

Wait what?

I was confused. Wasn't doing stuff... the job?

Turns out, at Atlan, every project needs:

- A problem/solution statement
- A clear metric it will move
- A process diagram

No exceptions.

My first attempt? Painful.

I sat for 3 hours trying to write a P/PS statement. 3 HOURS. 

For what used to take me 3 minutes to just start doing.

Felt like I was wasting time. Doing nothing.

Then something clicked.

Those 3 hours of thinking saved 30 hours of rework.

Now my split looks like (still WIP):
- 80% thinking
- 20% doing

The result? I do "slightly" better work in half the time.

My manager put it perfectly:

"Deep thinking takes time & willingness to question. 
First principles thinking takes time to develop. 
A powerful habit to incorporate is to make time to think."

Here's what "slowing down" taught me:

The best PMs aren't the fastest typers.

They're the deepest thinkers.

We've been trained to mistake motion for progress. 
But motion without direction is just expensive wandering.

Sometimes the most productive thing you can do is close your laptop and think.

What percentage of your day is thinking vs doing?

p.s. If your calendar is full of "doing" meetings, you might be stuck in the same trap I was :)

first_line: I quit my job with zero backup plan.
post_full:
I quit my job with zero backup plan.

Everyone said I was insane.

3 months later, I realized they were wrong about everything.

See, by January 2025, I was done:
- Mentally fried
- Living on autopilot
- Couldn't give 100% at Springworks

So I did what "responsible adults" never do.

I walked away.

- No next job lined up. 
- No 12-month runway saved.
- No side hustle to fall back on. 

Just... nothing.

My savings dropped 𝟰𝟬%. But, my anxiety dropped 𝟵𝟬%.

For the first time in my life, my calendar was empty.

And something strange happened. I became the happiest I'd ever been.

- Random coffee at 2pm? Sure. 
- Reading for 6 hours straight? Why not. 
- Deep diving into AI with no agenda? Absolutely.

Then I did something even crazier.

A, 10-day Vipassana retreat:
- No phone
- No talking
- 4:30am wake-ups
- 10 hours of meditation daily

The hardest thing I've ever done. Also the most transformative.

Here's what 3 months of "nothing" taught me:

The space between your last thing and your next thing?

That's not emptiness. 
That's where you find yourself again.

We're so obsessed with the next milestone that we forget:

Sometimes not knowing 𝗜𝗦 the plan.

If you can "afford" to take a break, do it. 

Not a vacation. Not a sabbatical with goals.

A real break. Where nothing is planned and everything is possible.

It might just be the most productive thing you never do.

p.s. Take this post as your sign to take a break from work :)

p.p.s First day at the Vipassana center 👇🏻

first_line: Last week was a huge win for me....
post_full:
Last week was a huge win for me....

I've been in SF for the past 3 months, grinding pretty much every day to build a strong HR community 

As I leave in a few days, I wanted to organize one last meet-up with all the amazing HR folks I've met so far :)

I am happy to report that it went AMAZING!!

It was the first-ever event where either Sandeep Talukdar or I used 0 LinkedIn invites to invite people.

(I kid you not; I now know you can only send 200 invites per week on LinkedIn)

To keep the community engaged, we had 2 group discussions:

1. Flexible hours: Boosts productivity or encourages procrastination
2. Office perks: Engaging or gimmicky.

When we first came here, building a community seemed such a huge task, I always wondered why someone would want to come down and meet us.

Weird, how the universe works.

From self-doubt to seeing so many repeated faces who've helped, showed up, and invited more people to our event.

A huge shoutout to the following people for always helping and showing up

Tipu Purkayastha, Jocelyn Thompson, SPHR, Janna Harrell, Kevin Stapp, Violet Selznick, Yan Gelman, Lisah Barry and Anurag Dixit

first_line: 2 weeks back, I met an old lady in Berkley...
post_full:
2 weeks back, I met an old lady in Berkley...

She was lost and had been walking for 50 minutes to figure out where she lived

She was distraught, tired, and 100% puzzled

I met her while I was crossing the road to buy some ice cream

I started talking to her to figure out where she stayed and tried my best to help

However, she couldn't trust me, and whenever I probed further she would ask me to leave

She kept repeating "This is my neighborhood, I have lived here for 22 years"

I tried multiple times to help her but to no avail

She just asked how she could reach Hearst and I gave her directions

Cut to 30 minutes later, after I'm done with my ice cream shopping

I took the same route to see if she made it, but as fate would have it, she unfortunately did not :(

No doubt she was lost

So for the next 45 minutes, I tried convincing her to trust me

I politely asked her to check her pockets and see if she had a phone, a card, or maybe a number to call

After tons of pleading and requesting, she emptied her pockets:
- I found a Berkley PD card
- An illegible receipt
- Keys

The saving grace? The Berkley PD card had a number and an address written on it.

We googled to see how far the address was and it said 2 miles.

She couldn't believe it.

I tried showing her photos of the address and she said well yeah this looks slightly familiar.

To me, it felt like she had early signs of dementia, so the best course of action would be to call Berkley PD

We finally did, and the police showed up in the next 10 minutes.

Interestingly, they already knew the context as the same thing happened 1 day before.

So happy everything turned out fine 🙏🏻

It's weird how the world works sometimes

p.s. This is not a Samaritan post, but a reflection on how the tech world is moving so fast, yet nobody is solving this.

p.p.s I'm not medically diagnosing her, that was my first reaction in the moment

first_line: It's so hard to be productive all the time...
post_full:
It's so hard to be productive all the time...

These past 2 weeks, I've worked my ass off in SF

Many people have asked me what I have seen here so far

When I tell them not much, they're usually surprised 😅

I think people just see the fancier version of our time here

But, what they don't see:
- Lows when people say this problem isn't worth solving
- Meeting 4-5 people daily, attending events 
- Cleaning, cooking, household chores
- Walking upwards of 8 Kms daily
- A lot of self-doubt

Don't get me wrong, I'm enjoying every bit of my time here

Though grinding daily is hard, like really hard

I'm a huge fan of what Naval Ravikant says:

Knowledge workers function like athletes - train, and sprint, then rest and reassess

So at this time, I'm training and sprinting 💪🏻

Today has been another day with a lot of productive meetings:

I started my day at 8 am 

I had a meeting with Pranav, and we spoke about what he thinks of AI in customer support:

- They've built an internal chatbot to manage customer support queries
- The need for an AI tool becomes imp when you've 10+ customer support reps 
- Dealing with nuances is critical, and the integrations with different sources of info like Discord, Slack, Intercom, etc should be stitched correctly.

In the afternoon, met Daniel today and discussed what he thinks about AI in customer support:

- Most orgs talk to customers using Slack connect channels, so can Albus read, categorize, and suggest answers by pinging the POC
- As Albus is integrated with all org docs and tools, can it suggest documentation changes when it finds conflicting information
- The overall idea is to improve documentation and get analytics on questions that are asked multiple times
- This is relevant as smaller companies don't have their documentation in place, so if we can help them with that, they will get their Aha moment faster

Time has flown by so quickly, I'm honestly looking to relax and catch up on sleep this weekend 💤

Sandeep Talukdar and I are doing our 1st road trip this Sunday, super stoked about this 🚀

Here's to another successful week of learning and growth 🥂

p.s. saw Dolby's office today 🙌🏻

first_line: Day 11 in SF…
post_full:
Day 11 in SF…

I am in love with the architecture here…

Buildings are so beautiful and every day I take atleast 3-4 pictures

So what was on today’s menu?

Met Murali, who has been in the HR tech space for a very long time

- He mentioned how we can approach partnerships with companies like SAP, workday and the $$$ potential of such deals 

- He explained the nuances around positioning and why is it imp in this space 

- We also got a master class on GDPR compliance 

Next stop was Yan, luckily we had to meet him just across the road

(Less travel today 🥹)

- We discussed how can we approach justifying the value prop for Albus

- He asked us to equate time saved in man hours to actual $$$

- We also tried understanding the entire rewards and recognition space

The last meeting of the day was Tushar Dadlani, who leads product at Walmart

- We talked a lot about the challenges around selling to enterprises 

- What was surprising was teams internally are using multiple comm tools like Slack, MS teams, Zoom chat etc (so weird)

- They have deep guard rails like not being able to copy info from their Slack

- Finding the actual source of truth is hard because of so many independent systems

- Internal push towards consolidating and rather than tooling focus a lot more on problems 

Today was yet another day filled with learnings. 

Tomorrow afternoon is packed with 4 meetings (all in the city 🫡)

Lets gooo!

first_line: 53 miles 🚄
post_full:
53 miles 🚄

The distance I traveled today to meet 3 founders...

As we're in our 0-1 journey, we're doing all things that don't scale

(Paul Graham, I hope you're proud of me 🥹) 

The day for Sandeep Talukdar and me was pretty long

It involved a lot of walking, trains, buses, and 1 Lyft ride

We were out of our house by 8:45 am to catch a Caltrain to San Jose

2 buses later, we met Girish Redekar at 11 am

We discussed:
- Diff between demand harvesting (market has existing demand) and generation (market needs education)
- Better to link products to existing problems that people can relate to
- Use a multi-channel GTM approach and double down on what works
- Demand generation is 10X harder than  harvesting

At 1 pm, we met Sachin Gupta, we discussed:
- Target niche segments or specific integrations where there’s less competition, especially in AI space
- Focus on being the best at a particular function within a smaller market vs competing in a crowded space
- Find smaller integration partners and become the best at serving their customers
- Outbound works only when there is good marketing cover

Quick pit stop at Starbucks to grab a bite and then walked to another bus stop 

The last meeting of the day was with Bo LEI at 3:00 Pm

We discussed:
- Focus on building a single, dense use case that addresses a specific problem
- Avoid spreading too thin by trying to solve too many problems at once
- Leverage niche webinars or events as an effective early-stage GTM strategy

He was kind enough to drop us to a nearby train station which took us back to the San Jose train station.

1 Cal train and some walking later we reached home at 6:30 PM and were dead tired.

p.s. On the ride back home, I continued gathering my thoughts about why knowledge management sucks today 😅

first_line: Completed my first week in SF…
post_full:
Completed my first week in SF…

1 word to describe it: INTENSE

The last week has been immensely challenging:

- setting up a new base
- meeting 3-4 people daily
- doing 1 event at least for 3 days/week
- Cooking, cleaning, laundry and a lot more 

Finding momemntum is hard when you move to a new city

But people here have made it easier 🫡

Today has been super fun as well, my morning started with an early meeting with Tom Allegretti Jr. 

He shared some amazing insights: 

1. HR tech Consolidation: Companies want all-in-one HR tools to optimize costs and efficiency. (Pain killer vs vitamin)

2. Automation Hesitation: Small teams resist automating minor tasks due to tight budgets. (Economic downturn)

3. Integrated Solutions: Businesses prefer multifunctional tools over cheaper, single-purpose options. (More with less concept)

Ended the day by doing a founder mixer organised by Daivik Goel. 

The AI hype is real, most founders I spoke with were using AI to build something.

A key focus/ trend is around AI knowledge management for customer facing teams.

I am also writing an article around this, and if you want to read it lmk will share when done. 

Tomorrow is pretty long, travelling to 3 different cities for 3 meetings 🚀

Wish me luck (I need it) 

P.s over the weekend I visited Berkley and saw the Oppenheimer physics lab 🤯
